statement: A tuning fork vibrates at the musical note A₄ (440 Hz), modelled as an underdamped oscillator with natural angular frequency ω₀ = 2π × 440 rad/s and damping coefficient γ = 8.796 rad/s. (a) Compute the quality factor Q. (b) Find how many full oscillation cycles pass before the amplitude falls to 1/e of its initial value. (c) State the half-power bandwidth Δω of this resonator.

Q.prompt: Calculate the quality factor Q = ω₀ / γ.
Q.hint: Q is a dimensionless ratio comparing the oscillation frequency to the damping rate. A higher Q means the oscillator rings for longer.
commonMistakes: Using Q = ω₀ / (2γ) — the factor of 2 appears in some textbook conventions but not in this one
Inverting the ratio and computing Q = γ / ω₀
Using the frequency in Hz (440) instead of the angular frequency in rad/s

omega_d.prompt: Find the damped angular frequency ωd.
omega_d.hint: ωd = √(ω₀² − γ²/4). For high-Q oscillators ωd ≈ ω₀.
commonMistakes: Omitting the ÷4 factor: using ω₀² − γ² instead of ω₀² − γ²/4

t_e.prompt: Find the time t_e at which the amplitude first drops to A₀/e.
t_e.hint: The amplitude envelope is A₀ e^(−γt/2). Set this equal to A₀/e and solve for t.
commonMistakes: Solving e^(−γt) = 1/e instead of e^(−γt/2) = 1/e, giving t_e = 1/γ rather than 2/γ

n_e.prompt: How many complete oscillation cycles occur in time t_e?
n_e.hint: Divide t_e by the damped period T_d = 2π/ωd.
commonMistakes: Dividing by the undamped period 2π/ω₀ instead of the damped period 2π/ωd
Forgetting to convert angular frequency to cycles (missing the 2π)

delta_omega.prompt: State the half-power bandwidth Δω of this resonance peak.
delta_omega.hint: For a driven damped oscillator the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the power resonance curve equals γ. Equivalently, Δω = ω₀/Q.
commonMistakes: Using Δω = 2γ — double-counting because some derivations define the half-width as γ/2
Confusing bandwidth in Hz (Δf = γ/2π) with bandwidth in rad/s (Δω = γ)

finalAnswerProse: The half-power bandwidth of the resonance is Δω = γ = 8.796 rad/s (≈ 1.4 Hz). The quality factor Q ≈ 314, and the amplitude falls to 1/e after approximately Q/π ≈ 100 cycles.
walkthrough: The quality factor Q = ω₀/γ = 2π × 440 / 8.796 ≈ 314. This matches a good tuning fork — it rings for roughly a few seconds at 440 Hz. The amplitude envelope is A₀ e^(−γt/2). Setting e^(−γt_e/2) = e^(−1) gives t_e = 2/γ ≈ 0.227 s. In that time the fork completes n_e = t_e × f_d ≈ 0.227 × 440 ≈ 99.9 ≈ 100 cycles, in agreement with the rule of thumb Q/π. The half-power bandwidth of the resonance is Δω = γ = 8.796 rad/s; the resonance peak sits at ω₀ and falls to half its peak power at ω₀ ± γ/2. The two most common errors in Q calculations are using the wrong factor of 2 (writing Q = ω₀/(2γ)) and computing bandwidth in Hz without converting from rad/s. Remember: Δω in rad/s equals γ; the corresponding bandwidth in Hz is Δf = γ/(2π).
